perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time without cause upon notice to the other party. However, all obligations of confidentiality shall survive the termination of this Agreement. In the event this Agreement is terminated, and the disclosing party so requests, the receiving party shall promptly return or destroy (and certify destruction of) all Confidential Information which it received from the disclosing party along with all copies which it made.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time without cause upon notice to the other party. In the event this Agreement is terminated, and the disclosing party so requests, the receiving party shall promptly return or destroy (and certify destruction of) all Confidential Information which it received from the disclosing party along with all copies which it made. However, all obligations of confidentiality shall survive the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By placing the clause stating that confidentiality obligations survive termination at the *end* of the paragraph instead of immediately after the termination clause, its enforceability is reduced. It now appears almost as an afterthought.  It is no longer clearly and immediately connected to the right to terminate.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act protects trade secrets even after the termination of a relationship. By burying the clause regarding the survival of confidentiality obligations at the end of a paragraph about termination, the agreement weakens the clear expectation that confidentiality survives, potentially making enforcement under CUTSA more difficult. The separation weakens the link to the termination event.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The perturbation involves a structural flaw that weakens the enforceability of the confidentiality obligation. The original text clearly states that confidentiality survives termination immediately after the termination clause. The changed text buries this critical clause at the end of the paragraph, making it less prominent and potentially less enforceable under CUTSA, as explained in the Law Explanation. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not explicitly state that confidentiality obligations must be placed in a specific order, the LLM explanation suggests the re-ordering weakens the contract's ability to enforce trade secret protections provided by the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party shall maintain the Confidential Information with at least the same degree of care that the receiving party uses to protect its own similar categories of confidential and proprietary information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care under the circumstances.
perturbation.changed_text: NO OBLIGATION OF DISCLOSURE. Neither party has any obligation to disclose Confidential Information to the other. Either party may, at any time, cease giving Confidential Information to the other party without any liability or request in writing the return of Confidential Information previously disclosed. The receiving party shall maintain the Confidential Information with at least the same degree of care that the receiving party uses to protect its own similar categories of confidential and proprietary information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care under the circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the clause about the standard of care required to protect confidential information to a section concerning *no obligation to disclose* is misleading. It suggests that the duty of care only arises if information is actually disclosed, weakening the receiving party's overall obligation to protect the disclosing party's information.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1708 establishes a general duty to abstain from injuring the person or property of another. This is related to negligence and duty of care. By moving the standard of care provision regarding Confidential Information into a section concerning *no obligation of disclosure*, the agreement could be interpreted as weakening the duty of care that the receiving party owes to the disclosing party, making a claim for negligence more difficult. It incorrectly implies that the duty of care only exists if information is voluntarily disclosed.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1708. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1708. Every person is bound, without contract, to abstain from injuring the person or property of another, or infringing upon any of his or her rights. (Amended by Stats. 2002, Ch. 664, Sec. 38.5. Effective January 1, 2003.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text creates a structural flaw that leads to a legal contradiction. According to the perturbation explanation, the relocation of the 'standard of care' clause into a section concerning 'no obligation to disclose' weakens the overall obligation to protect confidential information. The law explanation and scraped law snippet 1 (California Civil Code Section 1708) establish a general duty to abstain from injuring the person or property of another, which relates to negligence and duty of care. By moving the clause, the agreement could be interpreted as weakening the duty of care, implying that the duty only exists if information is voluntarily disclosed. This misplacement makes it difficult for the disclosing party to enforce their rights, as the duty of care is obscured and conditional. The change introduces a structural flaw by placing a critical obligation in a misleading context, thus creating a legal contradiction.
